data.title: Graham to Republicans: Stephen Miller is not the problem
data.description:
Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.), a close ally of President Trump, is arguing to Republican colleagues who are critical of Stephen Miller that the senior White House adviser “is not the problem” and will be in Trump’s inner circle until the end of his administration.

“As to Stephen Miller, to my colleagues who believe you can convince Donald Trump that Stephen Miller is a liability for him, good luck with that. When this clock strikes 12 on the Trump era, there will be a few people walking out the door with Donald Trump. Stephen Miller will be in that group,” Graham said in a Fox News interview.

“So I want everybody to know on the Republican side that Stephen Miller is not the problem, sanctuary cities is the problem,” Graham said.

Graham’s comments were directed at GOP colleagues such as Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) and Sen. Rand Paul (Ky.), who have criticized Miller’s influence over Trump.

Tillis on Tuesday criticized Secretary of Homeland Security Kristi Noem and Miller for claiming that Alex Pretti, the 37-year-old nurse who was fatally shot in Minneapolis over the weekend, was a “domestic terrorist,” despite a lack of evidence to support their claims.

“Those two people told the president before they even had an incident report whatsoever that the person who died was a terrorist. That is amateur hour at its worst,” Tillis said about Noem and Miller.

Tillis also slammed Miller on the Senate floor earlier this month for asserting that the United States has a right to Greenland and that no country would pose a military challenge if the Trump administration took control of the vast mineral-rich territory.

“Mr. Miller said that [Greenland] should be part of the U.S. That is absurd,” Tillis declared on the Senate floor Jan. 7.

“I think it was an amateurish comment and something that a deputy chief of staff and senior policy adviser should not have taken the position on,” he added. “He doesn’t speak for the U.S. government.

Miller immediately labeled Pretti a “domestic terrorist” but didn’t provide any evidence to justify the claim.

In a social media post, he called Pretti a “would-be assassin” who “tried to murder federal law enforcement.”  

Paul, the chairman of the Senate Homeland Security Committee, questioned the rush to characterize Pretti before a full investigation could be completed and noted that most law enforcement chiefs wait for all the facts before drawing conclusions.

“I can’t recall ever hearing a police chief immediately describing the victim as a ‘domestic terrorist’ or a ‘would-be assassin.’ For calm to be restored, an independent investigation is the least that should be done,” Paul wrote, making a pointed reference to Noem and Miller.

data.title: GOP, Democratic lawmakers ask for Trump meeting on immigration
data.description:
Reps. Brian Fitzpatrick (R-Pa.) and Thomas Souzzi (D-N.Y.) sent a Tuesday letter to President Trump requesting a meeting to address the public’s growing “rejection” of immigration enforcement efforts taking place across the country.

The letter from Fitzpatrick and Souzzi, who co-chair the bipartisan Problem Solver Caucus that works across party lines to resolve political issues, comes amid an uproar in Congress over the killing of a protestor on Saturday in Minneapolis by a Border Patrol agent. 

“The tragedy in Minneapolis and the looming federal government shutdown demand that Congress and the White House, Democrats and Republicans, finally work together to address the immigration and border security issues that have festered for too long,” the co-chairs wrote in their letter to Trump. 

“Decades of legislative branch gridlock and executive branch missteps have contributed to anxiety, fear, and now anger and mistrust. Let’s seize this moment to act together, in a bipartisan manner, for the good of our nation,” they added.

Both of the lawmakers touted the president’s “success in securing our border” but said the work he’s done is being “overshadowed” by a disdain for interior immigration enforcement. 

Their letter follows the death of two U.S. citizens in Minneapolis, Renee Good and Alex Pretti, at the hands of federal law enforcement personnel sent to Minnesota as part of a deportation crackdown. Local leaders and elected officials in Washington have called for independent investigations into the shootings.

On Tuesday, Fitzpatrick and Souzzi urged the president to require that all federal law enforcement-involved shooting incidents be subject to an independent investigation.

Additional requests included only allowing federal law enforcement officers to engage in enforcement actions for which they are properly trained and providing legal protection to immigrants while modernizing the legal immigration system.

“If we work together, there will be dramatically less need for enforcement, and we can focus on those violent criminals that must be deported, and on the positive contributions of the millions of immigrants who work hard, raise families, work together with law enforcement, and who contribute so much to our nation, as have so many generations of immigrants who came before them,” the two wrote. 

“Now is the time to come together: to lower the temperature, reject reckless rhetoric, condemn violence and hatred in all their forms, and lead with respect, restraint, and above all, humanity — for one another, and for the rights and institutions that hold our society together,” they added.

data.title: Inside the trillion-dollar fraud that is funding US enemies
data.description:
The Government Accountability Office just confirmed what criminals already know.

Earlier this month, the government’s own watchdog reported that federal agencies still can’t determine whether their anti-fraud efforts are working — a decade after the framework requiring them to do so became law. One recommended fix: make the Social Security Administration share death data with the Treasury so we stop paying dead people. 

This would help close a loophole that organized criminals exploit to steal $115 million per hour from U.S. taxpayers right now, according to fraud detection models run by my organization. That’s $115 million every single hour — not annually, not monthly, but hourly. That comes to approximately $1 trillion a year bleeding out of federal programs. 

Seventy percent of that money lands overseas within days, moving through the same financial pipelines used for drug trafficking, sanctions evasion and terrorism finance.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has said his department is investigating allegations that taxpayer funds tied to Minnesota fraud schemes were diverted through overseas transfers, including to Somalia, and may have ended up with al-Shabaab, the al Qaeda affiliate. But this is just a small piece of a much larger pie. During the pandemic, investigators traced stolen relief funds to Russian and Chinese state-backed networks and to organized crime rings in Nigeria and Iran.

This is not waste. It is not a bureaucratic error. It is industrial-scale theft, by which U.S. taxpayers are bankrolling our own destruction.

The U.S. government is the easiest mark in the world, and criminal networks know it. Foreign adversaries know it. The only people who didn’t fully grasp the scale were ourselves.

That may finally be changing. The Department of Justice just launched a new Division for National Fraud Enforcement. So far, 1,750 subpoenas have been issued and 98 defendants charged. They will find fraud in every state, in every major benefit program. 

But here’s the hard truth no one in Washington wants to say: Enforcement alone will not fix a system still designed to be exploited. 

These discoveries weren’t made by sophisticated oversight systems. They were made by people who showed up and verified reality before the money went out. A YouTuber and a whistleblower did what billion-dollar systems couldn’t. In December, Nick Shirley walked up to daycare centers in Minnesota that had been collecting millions in taxpayer subsidies. All he found were locked doors and empty rooms with no children. The video hit 100 million views.

In Maine, a whistleblower flagged a Medicaid provider now under federal investigation; the state has suspended payments after $28.8 million flowed to the company over five years. In California, the state auditor identified $4 billion in questionable Medi-Cal payments and unemployment systems that still haven’t fixed the vulnerabilities that let criminals steal, by my estimate, $32 billion during COVID.

So how did a guy with a phone and a whistleblower checking service logs find what the government couldn’t? Because he actually checked. The government, in contrast, just pays first and asks questions never.

Self-attestation is the flaw that is costing us a trillion dollars. Providers tell the government who they are and bill for services. Payment goes out. Verification comes later, if at all, that any services were delivered. By the time audits happen, the money is gone, the shell companies are dissolved and the criminals are operating under new names in new states. 

Criminal networks understand this better than most bureaucrats do. They trade hit-lists ranking states by weakest ID verification, fastest payouts and lowest audit risk. Minnesota has been on those lists for years. So have California, New York and Massachusetts.

We need to stop pointing fingers at each other and start pointing them at the criminal networks draining our Treasury. They don’t care about our divisions — they exploit them 

When insiders start stealing, it shows the system has already collapsed. The schemes have become so easy that government employees are now participating. In Georgia, two state legislators have been charged with filing fraudulent pandemic unemployment claims, lying about their employment status to collect benefits they weren’t entitled to while serving in office. 

The fix exists; the will doesn’t. Verify identity before payment. Confirm services were delivered before releasing funds. Don’t send taxpayer dollars out the door on trust alone. 

Banks do this in milliseconds. Retailers do it. Credit card companies do it. The technology exists. The data exists. What has been missing is the political momentum to fix systems that were never built for a world of transnational digital fraud. 

And let’s be clear: Fraud prevention is not benefit denial; it is benefit protection. When money is stolen, it doesn’t help families. It doesn’t feed children or house the homeless. It disappears overseas, funding criminals and terrorists while Americans in need get nothing. 

The new task force is essential. Prosecutors must pursue criminals aggressively. But prosecutions will always come after the money is gone. Minnesota’s $9 billion scandal is just the tip of a much bigger iceberg. If we want to stop losing $115 million per hour, prevention has to come first. 

A government that verifies identity only after money leaves the Treasury is not compassionate. It is negligent. The American grift has gone on long enough. The government says it is done being the mark. Now it has to prove it.

Haywood Talcove is CEO of LexisNexis Risk Solutions Government, where he works with more than 9,000 federal, state and local agencies on fraud prevention and identity verification. He has testified before Congress twice on government fraud and accompanied federal law enforcement on fraud investigations nationwide.

data.title: Rubio warns of military action against new Venezuela leaders if goals not met
data.description:
Secretary of State Marco Rubio went off script in his opening statement to senators during a hearing on the administration’s plans for Venezuela after ousting its longtime leader.

In prepared remarks, he warned lawmakers that the Trump administration is ready to take new military action against Venezuela if the country’s interim leadership defies U.S. expectations. 

“We are prepared to use force to ensure maximum cooperation if other methods fail,” Rubio wrote in prepared remarks submitted to the committee, according to The Associated Press.

“It is our hope that this will not prove necessary, but we will never shy away from our duty to the American people and our mission in this hemisphere.”

Rubio appeared for a hearing of the Senate Foreign Relations Committee on U.S. policy toward Venezuela, coming less than a month since President Trump launched an audacious military operation capturing Venezuela’s President Nicolas Maduro and claiming the country’s oil industry. 

Rubio’s appearance before the committee is the first time a senior administration official will publicly testify on Trump’s controversial policy in Latin America, launching a major, months-long military operation against suspected drug trafficking boats and capturing the Venezuelan leader.

Rubio, who also serves as Trump’s National Security Advisor, defended Trump’s decision to remove Maduro to face drug trafficking charges in the U.S. In his prepared remarks, he argued for continuing deadly military strikes on boats suspected of drug smuggling, seizing sanctioned tankers carrying Venezuelan oil and rejected allegations that Trump is violating the constitution by taking such actions.

“There is no war against Venezuela, and we did not occupy a country,” he said. “There are no U.S. troops on the ground. This was an operation to aid law enforcement.”

During his unscripted opening remarks, he said elections in Venezuela were the “end game,” but said the road there would not be easy.

“The end state here is we want a we want to reach a phase of transition where we are left with a friendly, stable, prosperous Venezuela — and democratic, in which all elements of society are represented in free and fair elections,” he said.

Republicans have largely stood behind Trump’s push to exert greater military force in the Western Hemisphere, although a move by Democrats to constrain the president’s ability to launch new military operations has gained some GOP support. 

Five Republicans joined all Democrats earlier this month to advance a war powers resolution that would require the president to come to Congress for authorization for further military action against Venezuela. But Republicans backed off that effort in a second vote, with two GOP senators voting against a final vote on the resolution. 

Sen. Todd Young (R-Ind.), a member of the Senate Foreign Relations Committee, explained his decision to flip his vote against the war powers resolution, saying he received a commitment from Trump that the president would come to Congress to ask for a formal authorization if “American forces were needed in major military operations in Venezuela.”

Sen. Jeanne Shaheen (D-N.H.), the ranking member on the committee, pushed back on Rubio, saying in prepared remarks that cooperation from Maduro’s deputy, now Venezuela’s interim president Delcy Rodriguez, is “tactical and temporary.” 

Rodriquez on Sunday said she has had “enough” of taking orders from the U.S.

Shaheen argued that Trump is spending “billions” on Venezuela, turning away from focusing on American issues of affordability and, through the administration’s funding cuts, weakening America’s security apparatus. 

“From Venezuela to Europe, the United States is spending more, risking more and achieving less,” Shaheen said.

“And everyday Americans are paying the price, both at the grocery store and in a more chaotic and less safe world. That does not project strength. It hands our adversaries exactly what they want.”

data.title: Threats investigated by Capitol Police jump 57 percent
data.description:
The number of threats investigated by U.S. Capitol Police (USCP) grew by 57 percent in 2025, according to a new report from the agency.

Last year, the police force said their threat investigators examined nearly 15,000 potential threats made against lawmakers and their families and staff or against the Capitol itself — 5,000 more than in 2024. 

The number of threat investigations has increased in each of the past three years. The agency processed 9,474 cases in 2024, 8,008 in 2023 and 7,501 in 2022, the report found.

In addition to ensuring the security of lawmakers in Washington, D.C., Capitol Police coordinates with local law enforcement agencies across the country to protect members of Congress in their home districts.

“While security on Capitol Grounds has already improved, we have been strengthening our partnerships with law enforcement agencies across the country to keep the Members of Congress safe when they are away from Capitol Hill,” Capitol Police Chief Michael Sullivan said. “We want to make sure agencies have the resources they need to be able to enhance protection, which is critical to the democratic process.”

Building security became a central concern for the agency following the Jan. 6, 2021, attack on the Capitol. Five Capitol Police officers died after the riots — though none during the attack — and more than 140 were injured.

Former USCP Chief Tom Manger wrote in a 2023 statement that the agency made over “100 significant improvements” to the building. These included gaining legal authority to declare a state of emergency and solicit the help of the National Guard, equipment upgrades and specialized training, he added.

Additionally, the 24/7 Protective Intelligence Operations Center was launched in 2024 to receive and process reports for lawmakers. If additional investigation is needed, the center refers cases to Capitol Police’s Investigative Division, according to the report.

The memo also noted that “false sense of anonymity online” has also contributed to a rise in reports.

Several lawmakers have gone public about the threats made to their and their families’ lives. 

After participating in a video last year with other lawmakers that urged military servicemembers to defy “illegal orders,” Sen. Elissa Slotkin (D-Mich.) said Capitol Police put her on 24/7 security due to “a huge spike in death threats and intimidation.”

President Trump called the lawmakers’ comments “SEDITIOUS BEHAVIOUR, punishable by DEATH!” in a Truth Social post following the video’s release.

Sen. Mark Kelly (D-Ariz.), who has been under investigation over his participation in the video, said he and his family saw an increase in threats after Trump’s comments.

“Leadership climate is set at the top,” Slotkin told ABC in November. “So, if the president is saying things like that, you can imagine people on the ground, what they’re doing, the calls into our office … into our teams, the calling of police.”

White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt later walked back Trump’s comment, saying the president only wants the lawmakers to be “held accountable.”

data.title: Republicans speak out on Omar attack: 'Completely unacceptable'
data.description:
Republicans are speaking out against an attack on Rep. Ilhan Omar (D-Minn.) on Tuesday night, in which she was sprayed with an unknown substance during a town hall event.

“What happened at Rep. Ilhan Omar’s town hall — where she was sprayed with a substance during the event — is completely unacceptable. Regardless of political views, we should all agree this behavior crosses a line and cannot be tolerated,” Rep. Mike Lawler (R-N.Y.) said in a post on the social platform X Tuesday.

In footage of the Tuesday incident, a man stood and yelled at Omar while she was at a podium and pushing for Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem to step out of her role or face impeachment. The man sprayed Omar before being tackled to the ground.

Rep. Mark Alford (R-Mo.) condemned the attack in his own post on X, saying it was “plain wrong.”

“I fully condemn this kind of behavior toward ANY elected officials, regardless of their political affiliations or beliefs. I do not agree with @IlhanMN on most everything, but this is wrong,” he added.

Tuesday’s town hall incident followed weeks of tension in Minnesota over President Trump’s immigration agenda. Two people, Renee Good and Alex Pretti, have been fatally shot by federal immigration authorities in Minneapolis this month, events that have rattled the state.

Rep. Nancy Mace (R-S.C.), typically a GOP firebrand, also expressed her disapproval of the spraying of Omar.

“I am deeply disturbed to learn that Rep. Ilhan Omar was attacked at a town hall today. Regardless of how vehemently I disagree with her rhetoric – and I do – no elected official should face physical attacks. This is not who we are,” Mace posted on X Tuesday.

Without evidence, Trump has suggested Omar staged the spraying incident. The Minnesota Democrat has become a frequent target of the president.

“I don’t think about her. I think she’s a fraud,” Trump told ABC News. “She probably had herself sprayed, knowing her.”

data.title: Ecuador says ICE officer tried to enter consulate in Minneapolis
data.description:
The Ecuadorian Ministry of Foreign Affairs said Tuesday that an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer attempted to enter the South American nation’s consulate in Minneapolis.

The ministry wrote on social platform X that the officer attempted to gain entry to the facility at roughly 11 a.m. CST. Consulate officials “immediately” prevented the immigration official from entering, thereby “ensuring the protection of the Ecuadorians who were at the headquarters at that moment and activating the emergency protocols issued by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Human Mobility.”

The officials also noted the nation’s government presented a “note of protest” to the U.S. Embassy in Quito, Ecuador. 

The Hill has reached out to ICE, the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and the State Department for comment.

The incident comes amid the Trump administration’s immigration enforcement operation in Minneapolis. The administration surged ICE and Border Patrol personnel to the city earlier this month.

Local and state officials have pushed back against the crackdown in the wake of the fatal shootings of Renee Good by an ICE officer on Jan. 7 and Alex Pretti by Border Patrol agents on Saturday. Both were 37-year-old U.S. citizens. 

White House border czar Tom Homan, who President Trump sent to Minneapolis on Monday to oversee the continued operation after U.S. Border Patrol commander Greg Bovino departed the area, met with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D) on Tuesday.

“Governor Walz met with Tom Homan this morning and reiterated Minnesota’s priorities: impartial investigations into the Minneapolis shootings involving federal agents, a swift, significant reduction in the number of federal forces in Minnesota, and an end to the campaign of retribution against Minnesota,” Walz’s office said in a statement to NewsNation, The Hill’s sister network. 

“The Governor and Homan agreed on the need for an ongoing dialogue and will continue working toward those goals, which the President also agreed to yesterday,” his office added. ” The Governor tasked the Minnesota Department of Public Safety as the primary liaison to Homan to ensure these goals are met.”

Trump has largely defended his administration’s moves in the North Star State, despite rising criticism of DHS Secretary Kristi Noem and the department’s use of force in the state. Democrats have threatened to launch impeachment proceedings against Noem if she is not removed or agrees to resign.

data.title: Trump tells Iran 'time is running out' for deal: 'Next attack will be far worse'
data.description:
President Trump on Wednesday threatened Iran with another attack that would be “far worse” than the three strikes last summer.

“Hopefully Iran will quickly ‘Come to the Table’ and negotiate a fair and equitable deal – NO NUCLEAR WEAPONS – one that is good for all parties. Time is running out, it is truly of the essence!” the president wrote in a Truth Social post.

“As I told Iran once before, MAKE A DEAL! They didn’t, and there was “Operation Midnight Hammer,” a major destruction of Iran. The next attack will be far worse! Don’t make that happen again. Thank you for your attention to this matter!” he added.

Last June, the United States struck three Iranian nuclear sites following lethal strikes led by Israel with hopes of dismantling their nuclear weapons development programs. 

Trump has clashed further with leaders in Tehran by backing anti-government protests in the country, warning that any acts of deadly violence against demonstrators would be met with military intervention.

More than 6,000 individuals have been killed since the Iranian government’s crackdown on the protests began.

“A massive Armada is heading to Iran. It is moving quickly, with great power, enthusiasm, and purpose. It is a larger fleet, headed by the great Aircraft Carrier Abraham Lincoln, than that sent to Venezuela,” the president wrote in the Wednesday post. 

“Like with Venezuela, it is, ready, willing, and able to rapidly fulfill its mission, with speed and violence, if necessary,” he continued.

Countries in the Middle East are bracing for lethal attacks.

data.title: Beyond testosterone: The real crisis in men's health
data.description:
Officials at the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services are signaling that testosterone replacement therapy could soon be the centerpiece of a new government-backed initiative focusing on men’s health.  

There’s no question men face a growing crisis of wellbeing in America, evidenced by high rates of addiction, obesity and chronic disease among other afflictions. But prioritizing testosterone use to combat these problems misses the mark.  

Increasing testosterone in men ignores deeply rooted cultural and social factors at the center of this health emergency. To effect meaningful change, our nation’s public health leaders must take a broader view. The national conversation surrounding men’s health should start where the evidence is clear: Men are dying at a younger age than women, largely from preventable causes.

The U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention found that young men suffer higher rates of heart attacks than women. Research also shows men are more likely to abuse alcohol and develop substance use disorders. Help exists, but the majority of men don’t seek it.  

More than 50 percent of men avoid regular medical screenings. For men of color, the number jumps to more than 60 percent. Men are often in the dark when it comes to their blood pressure and cholesterol levels which can yield early detection of many preventable health issues.  

Instead, many men only seek medical care when something is seriously wrong. Statistics show men have higher rates of hypertension, diabetes and heart disease — conditions that can lurk quietly until it’s too late. 

Men’s mental health is another silent killer. Twenty-five percent of young men struggle with loneliness. And nearly four times as many men die by suicide in America than women — yet men are less likely to reach out for help. Millions of men also suffer from undiagnosed depression and anxiety that can lead to unhealthy and destructive behaviors.  

These problems aren’t the result of lower testosterone levels — stigma associated with seeking help is often to blame. Many men are burdened by what the American Heart Association calls “misguided masculinity” — the notion that a man’s vulnerability can be seen or perceived as a sign of weakness.  

From an early age, boys are socialized to “tough it out” if they get hurt — a lesson that can translate into adult behaviors of ignoring pain, suppressing emotion and avoiding doctors to receive the help they need. 

To improve men’s health, we need to normalize mental health care for men and embed it in our schools, workplaces and communities. An investment in mental health programs would have a much bigger impact on men’s health than a wave of testosterone prescriptions.  

Preventative medicine is often discussed in the context of women’s health: Pap smears, mammograms, reproductive health screenings, immunizations and so forth. But men, too, need prevention-based care to encourage early diagnosis of potential deadly conditions. 

Ignoring one’s health and mental wellbeing has consequences. The data prove it. Life expectancy for men in the U.S. has declined in recent years, driven by so-called “deaths of despair” and other illnesses. A national men’s health agenda should focus on promoting health literacy and the importance of seeking care rather than looking to pharmaceuticals to provide simple answers to complex issues. 

Testosterone replacement therapy can be a legitimate medical treatment, especially for men with clinically low hormone levels when administered under physician supervision. But it’s not a cure-all remedy for the vast majority of health issues most men battle every day.  

We don’t need our public health institutions reinforcing the myth that men are simply in need of a chemical rebalancing. Improving men’s health requires that we redefine what strength looks like.

Men need to understand that stoicism isn’t a strategy when it comes to medical care. But this can’t happen unless we address the feelings of shame many men inherently feel when it comes to seeking medical attention. 

Rather than focus on testosterone, we need federally funded programs that promote preventative screenings and encourage men to talk openly about stress, trauma and mental health — and the importance of receiving treatment.   

Breast cancer awareness campaigns normalized open conversations for women and saved lives. These efforts were built on a commitment to change the narrative and remove the stigma that was once synonymous with a serious disease affecting women’s health. We need to approach men’s health issues the same way.  

The key to solving the men’s health crisis in America won’t come in a vial. It will take courage and resolve by those leading U.S. health policy to focus on the urgent work that must be done to tackle the real issues at stake. 

Lyndon Haviland, DrPH, MPH, is a distinguished scholar at the CUNY School of Public Health and Health Policy.

data.title: Watch live: Trump, Bessent host summit on newborn baby accounts
data.description:
President Trump and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent are holding a summit Wednesday morning with CEOs and investors ahead of the administration’s launch of “Trump accounts” for newborn babies.

Under the investment accounts, children in the U.S. with a Social Security number born between Jan. 1, 2025, and Dec. 31, 2028, will be given a stock market account with a one-time $1,000 deposit from the Treasury that will grow similarly to individual retirement accounts, or IRAs, according to the IRS.

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt, rapper Nicki Minaj, Sen. Ted Cruz (R-Texas), Rep. Jason Smith (R-Mo.) and Cheryl Hines, the wife of Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., are set to attend.

The event is scheduled to begin at 9:45 a.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.
